overview: Izzet Aggro in Standard leverages low-cost creatures and instant-speed burn to apply rapid pressure. Hexing Squelcher and Scalding Viper provide efficient early threats that synergize with direct damage spells like Burst Lightning. The goal is to close out games before opponents can stabilize with larger threats or sweepers.
win_conditions: Overwhelm opponents with early creature pressure from Scalding Viper and Hexing Squelcher
Finish games with direct damage from Burst Lightning and creature combat damage
Maintain board presence to force inefficient removal answers

keep_criteria: Hands with two lands and a one-drop like Scalding Viper
Hands containing Burst Lightning alongside a creature
Hands with early interaction and a curve into Hexing Squelcher
Keep any hand with 2-3 lands and at least one turn-1 or turn-2 play (e.g., Scalding Viper, Hexing Squelcher, or a cantrip/removal spell).
Prioritize keeping hands that can establish a board presence by turn 2.
mulligan_criteria: Hands lacking early creatures or burn spells
Hands with three or more lands
Hands missing a one-drop or two-drop threat
Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, three+ lands with no early plays, or hands lacking any way to interact or apply pressure.

early_game: Play Scalding Viper on turn one to establish immediate board presence
Use Burst Lightning to clear small blockers or push damage directly
Prioritize land drops to ensure consistent casting of early threats
Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or cheap threat if mana allows.
Turn 2: Play a land, cast a 2-drop threat or removal.
If opening with a Viper/Squelcher on turn 1, play land, cast creature, use its ETB to clear a potential blocker or hit face.
Sequence lands to ensure two-color availability by turn 2.
If drawing a hand with Burst Lightning, hold it for a key threat or to swing through a blocker.
mid_game: Deploy Hexing Squelcher to apply pressure and force removal
Sequence burn spells to maximize combat damage by clearing blockers efficiently
Maintain card advantage by trading efficiently rather than overcommitting resources
always have one mana open for interaction or finishing blows.
Manage hand by prioritizing threats over removal when ahead, and vice versa when behind.
Avoid overcommitting to the board without protection.
be mindful of opponent's recursion or mill strategies.
late_game: Push maximum damage with remaining creatures and burn
Look for ways to stabilize if the game extends, though the deck aims to end earlier
Use Burst Lightning to finish opponents who have stabilized with larger threats
High-impact threats like Hexing Squelcher, Scalding Viper, and Burst Lightning chain together to close out games.
Use removal to clear the final blocker, then swing with remaining threats.
If ahead, use direct damage to finish the opponent.
Go all-in when you have board advantage, the opponent has no sweepers or counterspells in hand, and you can pressure through their remaining interaction.
Also commit when you've out-resourced them and can trade favorably.

key_cards.name: Hexing Squelcher
key_cards.notes: Play on turn two to apply pressure; use its low cost to force inefficient removal or trade with larger creatures.

key_cards.name: Scalding Viper
key_cards.notes: Deploy immediately to establish an early threat; synergizes with burn spells to clear blockers or push lethal damage.

key_cards.name: Burst Lightning
key_cards.notes: Use to remove small blockers, finish opponents, or clear the way for creature attacks; sequence after combat if possible to maximize impact.

sequencing_tips: Hold Burst Lightning until after combat damage is declared to maximize total damage output
Play Scalding Viper before casting Hexing Squelcher to establish a curve that pressures opponents early
Use burn spells to clear blockers rather than trading creatures when you can push more damage
Common mistakes include overcommitting to the board without protection, holding too many removal spells when ahead, and mismanaging mana for interaction.
Be mindful of timing your Burst Lightning to both deal damage and draw a card efficiently.
Avoid casting spells into known counterspells without a plan.
Practice stack management and prioritize threats over removal when you have a clear path to victory.

matchups.advice: Push early damage relentlessly with Scalding Viper and Burst Lightning to prevent stabilization
Force early trades and use Burst Lightning to clear value engines before they scale
Prioritize board control and use Burst Lightning to clear opposing threats while pushing combat damage

Use cantrips to dig for answers or threats. Cast spells in response to known counterspells if you have priority tricks. Don't overcommit to the stack; use removal to clear their threats and pressure face. Consider sideboard cards that bypass counters or protect your spells.
Apply maximum pressure early before they stabilize. Use removal to eliminate their ramp sources or key threats. Don't let them get ahead in mana; trade efficiently and keep them on the back foot.

Win by out-resourcing and better sequencing. Prioritize card advantage and efficient removal. Trade creatures favorably and use removal to clear their threats before committing your own. Sideboard in additional interaction and threats to gain an edge.

common_threats: Early sweepers that clear your board
Counterspells that disrupt your burn sequence
Resilient creatures that outvalue your early pressure
Watch for sweepers (e.g., Cyclonic Rift, Wrath of God), counterspells, and targeted removal.
Note when opponents tap out for a single spell—they're likely holding interaction.
Track their graveyard for recursion threats and monitor their mana for potential ramp or combo pieces.

overview_evidence.evidence_span: Hexing Squelcher, Scalding Viper, Burst Lightning

win_conditions_evidence.evidence_span: Scalding Viper, Hexing Squelcher, Burst Lightning

sequencing_tips_evidence.evidence_span: Burst Lightning, Scalding Viper, Hexing Squelcher
